title: Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill gives three special gold medals called Congressional Gold Medals to the 1980 US men's Olympic ice hockey team. The team won a famous game against the Soviet Union that people call the "Miracle on Ice." The three gold medals will be put on display in museums for people to see. The government can also make and sell bronze copies of the medals to help pay for the costs of making them.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Miracle on Ice Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts. The United States Olympic Men's Ice Hockey Team played in the nineteen eighty Winter Olympics in Lake Placid, New York from February thirteenth to twenty-fourth, nineteen eighty. Team USA was made up of college players. They beat the Soviet Union team four to three on February twenty-second, nineteen eighty. The Soviet Union team had been the defending Olympic champions. The United States team roster included twenty players from states like Minnesota, Massachusetts, Wisconsin, and Michigan. The game between the United States and Soviet Union was shown on television with a tape delay on February twenty-second, nineteen eighty. It drew thirty-four million two hundred thousand average viewers. People called it a miracle because college hockey players beat a Soviet team that had won four gold medals in a row since nineteen sixty-four. Team USA then beat Finland four to one in their final game to win the gold medal. This was their first gold medal in men's ice hockey since nineteen sixty. Herb Brooks coached the nineteen eighty team. He had been cut from the nineteen sixty United States Olympic hockey team that won gold. Brooks was known as someone who motivated players. He built a team around hard work and believing in yourself and your teammates. The tournament happened when the United States was having problems with high prices, high gas costs, hostages in Iran, and tensions with the Soviet Union. The Soviet Union had invaded Afghanistan, which led to the United States boycotting the nineteen eighty Summer Olympics. The Miracle on Ice was a turning point for ice hockey in the United States. Sports Illustrated called the game the greatest sports moment of the twentieth century. The win brought ice hockey to newspaper front pages everywhere and opened doors for American players to join the National Hockey League. Since nineteen eighty, interest in ice hockey in the United States has grown greatly. Registrations with USA Hockey increased by nearly four hundred percent from one hundred thirty-six thousand in nineteen eighty to over five hundred sixty-four thousand today. The number of National Hockey League players from the United States grew from seventy-two in nineteen eighty to two hundred forty-five in twenty twenty-four.

sections.title: Section 3 Congressional Gold Medals
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore (the temporary president) of the Senate will arrange to give three gold medals to the members of the nineteen eighty United States Olympic Men's Ice Hockey Team. These medals recognize their extraordinary achievement when these college players beat the powerful Soviet hockey team in the historic Miracle on Ice. This victory lifted American spirits during the Cold War, inspired many generations, and changed ice hockey in the United States. The Secretary of the Treasury will make gold medals with suitable symbols, designs, and words as the Secretary decides. After the medals are awarded, one gold medal will go to the Lake Placid Olympic Center in Lake Placid, New York where it will be displayed and available for research. One gold medal will go to the United States Hockey Hall of Fame Museum in Eveleth, Minnesota where it will be displayed and available for research. One gold medal will go to the United States Olympic and Paralympic Museum in Colorado Springs, Colorado where it will be displayed and available for research.

sections.title: Section 4 Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary may make and sell bronze copies of the gold medals. The price must be enough to cover all costs including labor, materials, dies (tools used to make medals), use of machinery, and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5 Status of Medals
sections.text: Medals made under this Act are national medals for purposes of chapter fifty-one of title thirty-one of the United States Code (a section of federal law about medals). For purposes of sections fifty-one thirty-four and fifty-one thirty-six of title thirty-one of the United States Code, all medals made under this Act will be considered numismatic items (collectible coins and medals).

sections.title: Section 6 Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The government is authorized to charge the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund for whatever amounts are needed to pay for the costs of making the medals under this Act. Money received from selling the bronze duplicate medals will be put into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
